summary: DHH reacts to Elon Musk's acquisition of Twitter and the open letter written by Twitter employees demanding job security, benefits preservation, and dignified treatment. He argues the employees' demands are delusional given that Musk bought the company specifically to change how it operates, and that demanding no layoffs from a new owner who overpaid for an unprofitable company is fundamentally unrealistic. He characterizes the letter as performance theater rather than genuine negotiation, noting the employees have no leverage against someone like Musk. While extending some sympathy for the turbulent acquisition process, he ultimately views the demands as entitled and self-defeating.
key_points: Twitter employees' open letter demanding preserved headcount and benefits from a new owner of an unprofitable company is fundamentally delusional
No new owner who massively overpaid for a money-losing company would commit to zero layoffs or perpetual preservation of existing arrangements
The employees have no leverage and are using self-defeating negotiation tactics against someone like Musk
The open letter is better understood as performance theater than a serious attempt at negotiation
The entire point of the $44 billion acquisition was to run Twitter differently
money_quotes: You'd think they were giving an account of working for the mob. Not the reality of being highly-paid tech workers at an unprofitable social media platform that just got a new owner who bought the place to turn the ship around.
What new owner, especially one who just massively overpaid for a company that's been losing hundreds of millions of dollars lately, would commit not to layoff a single employee?
The whole point of Musk buying the Twitter was to run it differently! That's what the $44 billion offer yield in return: The power to make new plans for the company.
This is not just a delusional list, but also a self-defeating negotiation tactic. The Twitter employees who conceived of this letter have little to no leverage.
Much easier to explain the delusional demands as performance theater. Acting out the righteous belief that We, The Twitter Workers, Stand United Before The Great Peril.
That's not a free pass to throw entitled tantrums or demands.
key_insight: Employees demanding that a new owner preserve every job and benefit at an unprofitable company he bought specifically to overhaul reveals a fundamental disconnect between tech worker entitlement and business reality.
